title: Understanding Your I-20 and SEVIS Record
summary: Your I-20 is one of the most important documents you'll carry in the US. Here's what you need to know to keep it valid.

content.text: The I-20 (Certificate of Eligibility) is the document your school issues that proves your student status in the US. It's tied to your SEVIS record — a government database that tracks all international students and exchange visitors.
What is SEVIS?

SEVIS (Student and Exchange Visitor Information System) is a DHS database. Every F-1 student has a SEVIS record with a unique ID number (starts with N). Your school's International Student Office (ISO) manages your SEVIS record and must report changes like address updates, major changes, or enrollment status.
Key things on your I-20

items: Your SEVIS ID number (N00xxxxxxxx) — memorize this
Program start and end dates
Your school and program of study
Estimated costs and funding sources
Travel signature (page 2) — must be renewed every year

content.text: Always keep your I-20 valid and up to date. If you change your major, transfer schools, or need to extend your program, you MUST get a new I-20 BEFORE the change happens. Working with an expired or inaccurate I-20 can jeopardize your visa status.
When do you need your I-20?

items: Re-entering the US after international travel
Applying for OPT or CPT work authorization
Transferring schools
Applying for a driver's license (some states)
Proving your legal status for banking, housing, etc.

content.text: Before traveling internationally, visit your ISO to get a fresh travel signature on page 2 of your I-20. Signatures are valid for one year. Without a valid signature, you may have trouble re-entering the US.
What to do if you lose your I-20

items.title: Don't panic
items.description: Your SEVIS record still exists electronically. The paper I-20 can be reprinted.

items.title: Contact your ISO immediately
items.description: They can issue a replacement I-20 from the SEVIS system.

items.title: Keep digital copies
items.description: Always photograph or scan your I-20 and store it in cloud storage as a backup.

title: First-Week Campus Registration Guide
summary: Everything you need to do during your first week on campus: check-in, orientation, ID card, and more.

content.text: Your first week on campus can feel overwhelming, but most schools have a structured process for international students. Here's a checklist to make sure you don't miss anything.
Step-by-step registration

items.title: Check in with the International Student Office (ISO)
items.description: This is usually mandatory within the first few days. Bring your passport, I-20, and I-94 record. They'll verify your arrival and activate your SEVIS record.

items.title: Attend international student orientation
items.description: Most schools require this. You'll learn about campus resources, legal responsibilities, and meet other international students. Don't skip it!

items.title: Get your student ID card
items.description: Your student ID is your key to everything — library, gym, dining halls, building access, and student discounts around town.

items.title: Set up your university email and accounts
items.description: Activate your school email, learning management system (Canvas/Blackboard), and student portal. Your email is used for all official communication.

items.title: Register for classes (if not done online)
items.description: Meet with your academic advisor to finalize your course schedule. F-1 students must maintain full-time enrollment (usually 12+ credits for undergrad, 9+ for grad).

items.title: Get your campus parking permit or transit pass
items.description: Many schools offer discounted or free transit passes. If driving, register your car and get a parking permit early — they sell out.

content.text: F-1 students must be enrolled full-time every semester. Dropping below full-time status without ISO approval can put your visa in jeopardy. Always talk to your ISO before dropping a class.
Important campus resources to locate

items: International Student Office — your lifeline for visa and immigration questions
Student Health Center — where to go when you're sick (usually free or low-cost)
Counseling Center — free mental health support, often with multilingual counselors
Writing Center — free help with papers and assignments in English
Campus Safety / Police — save their number in your phone
Library — not just books: free printing, study rooms, laptop loans, and research help

content.text: Most campus services are included in your tuition and fees — you've already paid for them! Take advantage of free tutoring, career counseling, gym access, and student events.

title: Working in the US: CPT, OPT, and On-Campus Jobs
summary: F-1 students can work in the US, but there are strict rules. Here's what you need to know about CPT, OPT, and on-campus employment.

content.text: As an F-1 student, you're allowed to work in the US under certain conditions. Understanding your options early helps you plan internships, part-time jobs, and post-graduation employment.
On-Campus Employment
The easiest way to work. You can work up to 20 hours/week during the school year and 40 hours/week during breaks. No special authorization needed — just your valid F-1 status.

items: Library assistant, dining hall, research assistant, campus IT
Teaching assistant (TA) or grading positions
Campus bookstore, recreation center, student services

content.text: On-campus jobs fill up fast! Apply during the first week of the semester. Check your school's student employment portal or ask departments directly.
CPT (Curricular Practical Training)
CPT lets you do internships or co-ops that are part of your degree program. It must be authorized by your ISO before you start working.

items: Must be directly related to your major
Requires a job offer before applying
You must have been enrolled full-time for at least one academic year (some exceptions for grad students)
Can be part-time (≤20 hrs/week) or full-time during breaks
Using 12+ months of full-time CPT makes you ineligible for OPT

content.text: Never start working before your CPT authorization date. Working without authorization — even one day early — is a serious violation that can end your F-1 status.
OPT (Optional Practical Training)
OPT gives you 12 months of work authorization after graduation (or before, as pre-completion OPT). STEM degree holders can extend for an additional 24 months (STEM OPT).

items.title: Apply early
items.description: You can apply up to 90 days before graduation. USCIS processing takes 3-5 months, so don't wait.

items.title: Get your ISO's recommendation
items.description: Your ISO must recommend you in SEVIS before you can file with USCIS.

items.title: File Form I-765
items.description: Application for Employment Authorization. Filing fee is around $410 (check current amount).

items.title: Receive your EAD card
items.description: You cannot work until you have your Employment Authorization Document (EAD) in hand and your OPT start date has passed.

items.title: Report employment to your ISO
items.description: You must report your employer info within 10 days of starting a job. Unemployment gaps over 90 days (150 for STEM OPT) can end your status.

content.text: Quick Comparison

items.label: On-Campus
items.description: No special auth needed. Up to 20 hrs/week during semester. Easiest option but limited positions and lower pay.

items.label: CPT
items.description: For internships related to your degree. Must be authorized before start date. Time used may affect OPT eligibility.

items.label: OPT
items.description: 12 months post-graduation work (+ 24 months for STEM). Apply early! Most common path to full-time employment in the US.

content.text: Start planning for OPT at least 6 months before graduation. Attend career fairs, connect with your school's career center, and practice interviewing. Many employers start hiring interns and new grads a full semester in advance.
